coredata.dc:title: Chapter 19 Theranostic Magnetic Nanoparticles as Molecular Imaging Agents for siRNA Delivery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Handbook of Nanomaterials for Cancer Theranostics
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2018 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhe
Wang, Junqing
Liu, Gang
coredata.dc:description: RNA interference (RNAi) technology, which has gained tremendous attention in gene therapy, has been driven by nanoparticle-mediated short interfering RNA (siRNA) therapeutics. The rapidly developing field of RNAi-based treatment requires visualization and tracking of siRNA delivery to diseased lesion sites, and evaluating the gene-silencing efficiency with phenotype modulation (such as tumor volume reduction). The iron oxide-based, magnetic nanoparticles (IOMNPs)-based siRNA delivery platform holds great potential for delivering results in a real-time, non-invasive, biodegradable, low-toxicity and reproducible approach. This chapter summarizes the fundamental magnetic features of IOMNPs, and covers the surface coating and functionalization methods. In addition, the pharmacokinetics, targeting strategies, cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking, and toxicity of IOMNPs-mediated siRNA delivery are discussed. Also, important studies of the application for siRNA imaging based on the IOMNPs delivery platform are summarized, and the chapter concludes with a brief outlook on future developments in the approach to clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RNA interference (RNAi)
Magnetic resonance imaging
Small interfering RNAs (siRNAs)
Near-infrared
Iron oxide-based magnetic nanoparticle
Hydrodynamic diameter

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Surface Modification of IOMNPs
Primary Surface Coating and Functionalization With siRNA
Molecular Imaging of IOMNPs-Mediated siRNA Delivery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Key utilizations of IOMNPs in RNAi theranostic application.
An overview of different types of magnetic behavior.

(A) Schematic representation of the IOMNPs medicated T2 contrast enhancement. (B) The MR signals intensity of water and IOMNPs against time in T2 relaxation. (C) The key factors and parameters in measurement of R2.
Schematic illustration of the coercivity and size relations of IOMNPs.
Schematic illustration of the IOMNP system for magnetofection and magnetic fields controlled in vivo therapeutic delivery.
A summary of major synthetic approaches of IOMNPs.
Schematic illustration of the general methods for primary surface coating of IOMNPs.
Different strategies for siRNA molecules binding with functionalized IOMNPs.
The systemic delivery of IOMNP-siRNA complexes and targeting strategies for disease site approach.
Overview of the various clinically approved IOMNP formulations (Singh & Sahoo, 2014; Wang, 2011)

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 19
chapter.ce:title: Theranostic Magnetic Nanoparticles as Molecular Imaging Agents for siRNA Delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: RNA interference (RNAi) technology, which has gained tremendous attention in gene therapy, has been driven by nanoparticle-mediated short interfering RNA (siRNA) therapeutics. The rapidly developing field of RNAi-based treatment requires visualization and tracking of siRNA delivery to diseased lesion sites, and evaluating the gene-silencing efficiency with phenotype modulation (such as tumor volume reduction). The iron oxide-based, magnetic nanoparticles (IOMNPs)-based siRNA delivery platform holds great potential for delivering results in a real-time, non-invasive, biodegradable, low-toxicity and reproducible approach. This chapter summarizes the fundamental magnetic features of IOMNPs, and covers the surface coating and functionalization methods. In addition, the pharmacokinetics, targeting strategies, cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking, and toxicity of IOMNPs-mediated siRNA delivery are discussed. Also, important studies of the application for siRNA imaging based on the IOMNPs delivery platform are summarized, and the chapter concludes with a brief outlook on future developments in the approach to clinical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Address correspondence to junqing@me.com and gangliu.cmitm@xmu.edu.cn. This study was supported by the Major State Basic Research Development Program of China (Grant Nos. 2017YFA0205201, 2014CB744503 and 2013CB733802), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant Nos. 81422023, 81371596, 51273165, U1705281 and U1505221), the Key Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (Grant No. 212149), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (Grant No. 2013121039 and 20720150141), and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (Grant No. NCET-13-0502).

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of RNA-mediated inhibition of protein expression in plants and fungi in the early 1900s (Napoli, Lemieux, & Jorgensen, 1990; Romano & Macino, 1992), and later the important study of RNA interference (RNAi) in Caenorhabditis elegans in 1998 (Fire et al., 1998), small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) have been exploited as a powerful research tool, and the promising therapeutic agents for post-transcriptional protein-coding gene knockdown in diseased cells (Ryther, Flynt, Phillips, & Patton, 2005). Their intriguing features include: (1) the unique specificity (sequence-specific binding of base pairs between siRNA and target messenger RNAs); (2) high efficiency with a catalytic nature (RNAi affects amplification via an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase mediated secondary siRNA replication process) (Lipardi, Wei, & Paterson, 2001; Sijen et al., 2001); and (3) wide applicability (in principle, siRNAs can be designed and synthesized to knock down any target gene with a complementary sequence). These features are highly beneficial for the personalized novel treatment of various diseases, especially in use against previously undruggable targets, for example, the messenger RNAs of oncogene proteins (Wu, Lopez-Berestein, Calin, & Sood, 2014).

To date, siRNA-based therapeutics have moved into clinical trials since the first-in-human study of bevasiranib (Cand5) for the treatment of wet age-related macular degeneration in 2004 (OPKO Health, Inc., n.d.; Ozcan, Ozpolat, Coleman, Sood, & Lopez-Berestein, 2015). Despite some encouraging success of these clinical trials, a number of challenges are still faced by RNAi-based therapeutics in systemic use. For example, delivery efficiency of siRNAs or other small therapeutic RNAs to target tissue is impeded by many factors, including nuclease degradation in plasma, and rapid systemic elimination of naked siRNA (Alexis, Pridgen, Molnar, & Farokhzad, 2008); entrapment of nano-sized carriers in the reticuloendothelial system and phagocytized by phagocytic cells (Wang, Lu, Wientjes, & Au, 2010); the vascular endothelial barrier in many tissues, and the resistance of extracellular matrix and intracellular release via endosome escape (Haussecker, 2014). Furthermore, the siRNA-based therapeutics have raised safety concerns related to adverse side effects, such as liver toxicity, which can be induced by the saturation effect that occurs when introduced foreign siRNAs compete with endogenous RNA through the miRNA regulatory pathway (Grimm et al., 2006); the induction of innate immune responses caused by irrational design of the siRNA sequence (Hornung et al., 2005; Reynolds et al., 2006); and the unpredictable phenotypic toxicity of off-target effects induced by siRNA (Fedorov et al., 2006). Therefore, it is necessary to overcome these major obstacles of unwanted biodistribution, insufficient therapeutic efficiency, and undesired toxicity for the clinical translation of siRNA-based therapeutics.

In this account, IOMNPs can be rendered as an ideal theranostic nanoplatform for real time in vivo monitoring of siRNA delivery, following the treatment responses to evaluate and address the translational challenges, thereby accelerating the progress of RNAi therapeutics development. This chapter focuses on recent conspectus in the synthesis and surface functionalization of IOMNPs for targeted imaging and magnetic-guided siRNA delivery. This chapter also discusses the pharmacokinetics, targeting approaches, cellular internalization, intracellular trafficking, and toxicity of IOMNPs-mediated siRNA delivery. In addition, some prominent reports of applications for siRNA imaging based on the IOMNPs delivery platform are outlined.

The application of magnetic nanoparticles in a variety of medical practices has been witnessed in past decades. The novelty of using these nanoparticles in vitro/in vivo primarily depends on their nanoscale superparamagnetic behavior being easily manipulated by using an applied magnetic field. Among various magnetic nanomaterials that consist of magnetic elements such as iron, nickel and cobalt; IOMNPs, including magnetite (Fe3O4) and its oxidized form maghemite (γ-Fe2O3), have been mostly exploited in the biomedical field due to their better biocompatibility and safety profile than other metallic-based magnetic nanomaterials. In regard to the biomedical application field, several important parameters are generally considered for shaping the nanoscale magnetism of magnetic nanoparticles, including the transverse relaxation rate (R2) or relaxivity coefficient (r2), coercivity (H c), specific loss powers (SLPs), and translational attractive force (F m).

Before the discussion of the above-mentioned parameters of IOMNPs, we recall some fundamental concepts of magnetism. The magnetism in materials is a class of physical phenomena that is medicated by the magnetic field, which arises from the electron magnetic moment in atoms (Fig. 2 ). Virtually all materials exhibit diamagnetism that can generate an opposed magnetic field to repeal the applied magnetic field (H), but they are usually very weak, and often shielded by other, stronger intrinsic magnetic effects, such as paramagnetism and ferromagnetism. The atoms in diamagnetic materials have no net magnetic moment when there is no external H applied. In contrast with diamagnetism, paramagnetism is a type of magnetic state in which paramagnetic materials can be attracted by the H due to the partially induced magnetic moment of atoms, which is linearly aligned in same direction as the H. These materials do not maintain their induced magnetic properties when the H is absent, because the direction of the magnetic moment is randomly oriented under the influence of the Néel temperature (T N). Comparably, superparamagnetism shows similar magnetic behavior to paramagnetism, which is raised from small, single-domain ferromagnetic/ferrimagnetic nanoparticles. Such nanoscale behavior can be retained, even when the temperature drops below the T N, because the energy required to undermine the magnetic ordering of a nanoparticle is close to ambient temperature.

One common magnetic behavior exhibited on permanent magnets is called ferromagnetism. Ferromagnetic materials stem from a parallel alignment of the atomic magnetic moments, and are maintained at a lowered energy state with a favorable tendency. As for ferrimagnetism, it is found in ionic compounds, and possesses a more complex crystal structure. Ferrimagnetism exhibits similar behavior to that of ferromagnetism, but holds different magnetic ordering within the structure. Such magnetic structure can be divided into two types of sub-lattices with unequal magnetic moments, leading to a spontaneous net magnetization. The populations of atoms in these materials are grouped together into magnetic domains. In each domain, all atomic moments are aligned in the same direction. However, some domains with opposing magnetic moments weaken the overall magnetization of the material. As for antiferromagnetism, the magnetic moments of neighboring atoms in the materials are aligned in an orderly manner in the opposite direction. In the absence of external H, they have similar magnetic properties to those of paramagnetism, as the atomic moments cancel out the total magnetization.

IOMNPs with enhanced magnetic parameters are favorable theranostic agents for high sensitivity MRIs, efficient magnetofection, magnetothermal-based hyperthermia therapy, and controlled therapeutics release. At first, IOMNPs are specifically designed as the T2/negative contrast agents for MRI applications. Because a secondary magnetic field of IOMNPs is generated by applying an external magnetic field (EMF), the transverse (spin-spin) relaxation process of protons in water molecules surrounding IOMNPs is agitated by local inhomogeneous magnetic moments, which results in a reduced transverse relaxation time (T2) of those surrounding protons. This effect leads to a negative enhancement by decreasing MR signal intensity, which is displayed as a darker T2-weighted image. The contrast enhancement ability of IOMNPs is defined in terms of its transverse relaxation rates, R2 (s−1) (Eq. 1) and the relaxivity coefficient (r2), a gradient of line given by R2 against concentrations of IOMNP (Fig. 3C). The R2 is measured as 1/T2, and the equation predicts that a high saturation magnetization (M S) and large particle diameter (r) are essential for a higher R2, and thus an enhanced T2 contrast image (Fig. 3C). And yet, the IOMNP coercivity (H c) is worthy of attention when particles’ size is increased for contrast enhancement. Once IOMNPs are under an external magnetic field (H), their induced magnetic moments are aligned along the H direction, and reach the M S. The coercivity is the strength of the applied magnetic field that required demagnetizing these ferromagnetic/ferrimagnetic particles, which is size-dependent (Fig. 4 ). Because the units of magnetic moments in particles are grouped in magnetic domains, this determines their magnetic behavior. The multiple magnetic domain particles can convert to single domain when their size decreases to a certain value, and further reduction of the particle size below a critical diameter can exhibit superparamagnetic characteristics, that is, H c = 0. Therefore, after the removal of the EMF, the remnant magnetization of IOMNPs can be readily demagnetized through thermal agitation at an ambient temperature. This favorable property avoids undesired magnetic dipole-induced aggregation of IOMNPs in liquid media, which provides them a unique advantage for biological application.
(1)

T 2

256

∅ ⋅

L r
Where T2 is transverse relaxation time, γ is the proton gyromagnetic ratio, M s is saturation magnetization, ∅ is the nanoparticle volume fraction, r is the nanoparticle core radius, D is the diffusion coefficient of water molecules, and L is the thickness of the nanoparticle surface coating (Koenig & Kellar, 1995).

In addition to the MR contrast imaging and drug release, IOMNPs-based therapeutic carriers can also be directed (to push or attract) by a designed magnet system for magnetofection and magnetic fields controlled in vivo therapeutic delivery (Fig. 5 ). The magnetic force experienced by a single IOMNP is defined by magnetic fields (F mag) (Eq. 2), which depends on the magnetic intensity (H), field gradient in space, particle size, saturation magnetization, and the position of the particle. It should be noted that particle size should remain small enough to retain the superparamagnetism.

Where a is the radius of the IOMNP, μ 0 is the vacuum magnetic permeability, χ is the magnetic susceptibility, H is the magnetic intensity, x = (x, y, z) denotes the position of the IOMNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Surface Modification of IOMNPs

ce:section.ce:para: In the biomedical filed, IOMNPs typically refer to spinel-structured magnetite (Fe3O4), maghemite (γ-Fe2O3) and their analogous ferrites (MO · Fe2O3 or MFe2O4, where the M is the divalent metal iron such as Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+). During the past five decades, the synthetic development of monodispersed and uniform sized IOMNPs has been intensively investigated (Schwertmann & Cornell, 2000). A variety of synthetic routes, including co-precipitation, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave irradiation, the sol-gel process, the microemulsion method, the electrochemical method, spray/laser pyrolysis, and sonolysis have been developed. Among these, to meet the requirements of the biomedical application; the uniform size distribution, the monodispersity, the particle size control, and the purity are crucial for in vivo biocompatibility, toxicity, and performance of the IOMNPs. Currently, the synthesis of IOMNPs via the alkaline co-precipitation in an aqueous solution, in microemulsions, and the thermal decomposition of iron organometallic precursors in non-aqueous media are mostly studied; and these are discussed in this section (Fig. 6 ). Other synthetic methods are less popular in respect to biomedical application, and have reviewed elsewhere (Laurent et al., 2008; Sun et al., 2014; Tartaj, Morales, Veintemillas-Verdaguer, Gonzalez-Carreno, & Serna, 2003).

The classical synthesis by alkaline co-precipitation in aqueous solution is a facile and inexpensive chemical pathway for mass production of IOMNPs. Fe3O4 and γ-Fe2O3 are generally obtained by co-precipitation of a specific amount of ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric (Fe3+) salts in a basic aqueous solution. For complete precipitation of Fe3O4, the reaction conditions, including the Fe2+: Fe3+ ratio, which is fixed at 1: 2, the pH should be controlled between 8 and 14, and the reaction needs to be processed in a non-oxidizing oxygen environment (Jolivet, Chaneac, & Tronc, 2004; Massart & Cabuil, 1987). However, the as-prepared Fe3O4 is not thermodynamically stable, and can be readily oxidized to form γ-Fe2O3 in air (Jolivet et al., 2004). In order to obtain the relatively stable IOMNPs, the intended oxidation of IOMNPs is usually performed after the initial formation of Fe3O4 (Massart, 1981). In addition, it is possible to tailor the particle size with a mean diameter ranging from 2 to 20nm, by modifying the reaction parameters, such as pH, ion concentration, reaction temperature, Fe2+: Fe3+ ratio, and the type of alkali salts (Babes, Denizot, Tanguy, Le Jeune, & Jallet, 1999; Jean-Pierre, 2000; Lopez-Lopez, Duran, Delgado, & Gonzalez-Caballero, 2005; Massart, 1981). Nonetheless, the size distribution of IOMNPs can hardly be controlled within a narrow range through co-precipitation in an aqueous system (Lu, Salabas, & Schuth, 2007), because two critical steps are involved in the formation of the IOMNPs: (1) nucleation and (2) crystal growth on the nuclei (Schwertmann & Cornell, 2000). Nucleation occurs when the critical supersaturation value is reached; then a growth of the nuclei spontaneously takes place. A number of kinetic factors govern this process (Boistelle & Astier, 1988; Etienne & Stéphane, 2012; LaMer & Dinegar, 1950). According to the LaMer mechanism and the Ostwald ripening phenomenon (Boistelle & Astier, 1988; LaMer & Dinegar, 1950), the periods of nucleation and crystal growth should be separated, and nucleation needs to be prevented during the process of crystal growth. A slowly controlled crystal growth, as well as other optimizations, are desirable for improving size distribution control and producing monodispersed IOMNPs (Etienne & Stéphane, 2012; Schwarzer & Peukert, 2004; Sugimoto, 2003).

Alternatively, the alkaline co-precipitation in microemulsion has been developed to overcome the shortcomings of the conventional co-precipitation method by achieving controlled homogeneity of size and morphology (Chin & Yaacob, 2007). This method is also applied to fabricate analogous ferrite nanoparticles (MFe2O4). A microemulsion system is a chemically stable isotropic dispersion of two immiscible liquids; for example, nanosized water droplets disperse in the oil phase (water-in-oil microemulsions), or vice versa, that is, oil-in-water microemulsions (Drmota, Koseli, & Žnidaršič, 2012). The interface between the two phases is stabilized by surfactants and co-surfactants, which provide a restriction effect that confines particle growth and agglomeration. In this method, IOMNPs can be simply prepared by combining two water-in-oil microemulsions, with microemulsion 1 containing metal salts and microemulsion 2 containing reducing agents (Boutonnet, Kizling, Stenius, & Maire, 1982; Boutonnet, Kizling, Touroude, Maire, & Stenius, 1991). The co-precipitation occurs in aqueous droplet-dimmers/aggregates due to the random collision between the mixtures of water-in-oil droplets. The microemulsion method takes advantage of using size-adjustable nano-droplets as nanoreactors to synthesize size-controlled IOMNPs with an average diameter from a few, to several hundred nanometers. The size of nano-droplets can also be controlled by modifying the water/oil/surfactant ratio (Feltin & Pileni, 1997; Muller & Muller, 1984). However, this method suffers from the drawbacks of poor crystalline particles (usually reduces the superparamagnetic properties) obtained from low-temperature synthesis, which could be improved by post-processing with thermal annealing (Mahmoudi, Sant, Wang, Laurent, & Sen, 2011). Moreover, large-scale production is also limited due to the massive quantities of oil required, and complicated post-processing for surface functionalization (Lin, Kim, El Haj, & Dobson, 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primary Surface Coating and Functionalization With siRNA
ce:section.ce:para: There are several potential problems, including immunogenicity (Bessis, GarciaCozar, & Boissier, 2004), carcinogenesis (Baum, Kustikova, Modlich, Li, & Fehse, 2006), insufficient siRNA loading capacity (Thomas, Ehrhardt, & Kay, 2003), toxin generation (Nayerossadat, Maedeh, & Ali, 2012), and complicated production processes, that limit the use of viral vectors for gene therapy (Bouard, Alazard-Dany, & Cosset, 2009). Like other inorganic-based gene carriers, IOMNP-based siRNA carriers have been increasingly studied in the past decade, and they are expected to become one of the non-viral carriers for replacing viral mediated siRNA delivery (Xing et al., 2014). Despite the fact that the monodispersed and uniformly-sized IOMNPs can be synthesized in organic media, they must be coated to achieve hydrophilicity, long-term stability, additional functionality (i.e., targeting and siRNA packaging), and modulation of r2 relaxivity for in vivo application. The surface coating of hydrophobic IOMNPs is often performed through the ligand exchange, using high-affinity hydrophilic ligands, or encapsulation with organic or inorganic materials (Fig. 7 ).

In ligand exchange methods, the ideal coating ligands should have a nucleophilicmoietyon on one end that binds the iron oxide surface (such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, thiol, and phosphate groups), and a hydrophilic functional group on the other end that is exposed to the biological environment. Due to the simple reaction approach and desirable thickness of the coating layer, many exchange ligands have been developed to meet the requirements of certain biomedical applications (Xie et al., 2011). For example, carboxylic acid, dopamine, and their analogues (e.g., dimercaptosuccinic acid and zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate (Xie et al., 2011)) are commonly used for compact surface coating and functionalization. In contrast, various kinds of polymeric ligands with a relatively large molecular weight are more widely used due to better stability and multi-functionality. Among these polymeric ligands, the FDA-approved poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), dextran, poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP), poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and their derivatives are favorable for in vivo application because of excellent biocompatibility. In addition, poly (ethylenimine) (PEI), an efficient gene delivery vector, is specifically utilized for metal surface coating and gene transfection.

The other strategy for surface coating is the encapsulation of particles within amphiphilic ligands, the hydrophilic polymer matrix, and inorganic materials. The self-assembling of amphiphilic ligands on the hydrophobic surface of the particles is based on the intercalations between the hydrophobic surfactants of particles and hydrophobic segments of amphiphilic ligands, namely hydrophobic interactions. Many amphiphilic copolymers have been used for coating the IOMNPs, such as PEG-phospholipid, tetradecylphosphonate, maleimide/methoxy-PEG-poly (lactide) (Nasongkla et al., 2006), polyoxyethylene 2 alkyl ether (Robinson et al., 2005), and so on. However, surface coating through the addition of amphiphilic copolymers can significantly increase the hydrodynamic diameter (HD), thus using amphiphilic oligomers (e.g., alpha-cyclodextrin) could be a desirable method of providing a thinner surface layer for the physiological environment (Wang, Wong, Teng, Lin, & Yang, 2003). In addition to the single-core encapsulation, multiple IOMNPs can also be embedded by copolymers to form a hydrophilic IOMNP-polymer complex particle. Numerous copolymers, including polystyrene-co-PEG, poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid)-co-PEG, and polystyrene-co-poly-(acrylic acid) have been used as polymer matrixes. Such hybridizations can produce large particle size, as well as increasing the T2 signal intensity (Neely et al., 2013).

In comparison with the organic ligands and polymers, silica (Si) and gold (Au)-based inorganic materials provide several advantages, including tunable shell thickness, better stability, and ease of surface functionalization for the surface coating of IOMNPs. Tetraethyl orthosilicate and tetramethyl orthosilicate are common silane coupling agents that are often used for silica coating of metal surfaces; the hydroxyl groups on the IOMNP surface are essential to initiate the oxane (Si-O-Metal) bridging via the Stöber process. For hydrophobic IOMNPs that lack hydroxyl groups on the surface, coating can be achieved by reverse microemulsion techniques (Yi, Lee, Papaefthymiou, & Ying, 2006). The silica-coated surface can be further modified by coupling with organo-functional silanes (amino-silane, epoxy-silane, vinyl-silane, etc.). Moreover, the coating of IOMNPs by a thin layer of gold is another attractive approach to enabling even more functionality toward sensing, drug delivery, and hyperthermia therapy. The preparation of a surface coating with gold can be adopted by the reverse micelle method (Lin et al., 2001), and the additional gold layer can be facilely functionalized using gold-thiol chemistry.

Based on the primary surface coating, siRNA molecules can be conjugated with IOMNPs through various strategies (Fig. 8 ). In general, two basic considerations need to be taken into account, (1) the siRNA loaded IOMNPs must remain physiologically stable until entering the cytoplasm; (2) the siRNA needs to be unpacked from the IOMNPs, and released from the endosome for the initiation of RNA-induced silencing complex activation. One of the attractive attributes of the siRNA delivery is that it relies on the electrostatic interactions between water-soluble cationic polymer-based IOMNPs and the siRNA anionic phosphate backbone. The linear/branched polyethylenimine PEI and their variations are widely-used, water-soluble cationic polymers that consist of repeating amine units, which can efficiently attach to the siRNA under physiological pH. In addition, high siRNA loading efficacy can be achieved by layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly of siRNA and cationic polymers on the surface of a single IOMNP based on its multilayered, core-shell structure. Furthermore, after the intracellular uptake of siRNA-cationic polymer-IOMNP complexes by endosomes, those amine groups are protonated by absorb protons, referred to as the proton-sponge effect, which leads to the spontaneous influx of water and Clˉ ions to balance the proton flux inside the endosomal vesicles (Sonawane, Szoka, & Verkman, 2003). This results in osmotic swelling, and the membrane rupture of endosomes with the subsequent leakage of its contents (siRNA with cationic polymer-based IOMNPs) into the cytosol (Akinc, Thomas, Klibanov, & Langer, 2005; Sonawane et al., 2003). Other alternative cationic polymers and lipids, including polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers (Taratula et al., 2011), poly-l-lysine (Cui et al., 2014), and Metafectene™ (Mykhaylyk et al., 2007), have also been reported to functionalize IOMNPs for electrostatic-based siRNA delivery.

In addition to using attractive electrostatic interaction, the siRNA molecules can also be attached on the surface of IOMNPs by either direct grafting or cross-linker-mediated immobilization, depending on the primary surface coating. In fact, the terminal site of siRNA molecules is often functionalized with the thiol group (SH-siRNA) for further conjugation. Thereby, direct grafting can be achieved by forming the thiolate bond between thiolated siRNA and gold-coated IOMNPs. Besides, it has been proposed that the intracellular SH-siRNAs can be released from the surface of gold-coated IOMNPs by exchanging SH-siRNA with glutathione (GSH, which is an important thiolcontaining antioxidant and highly abundant in most cells) in the cytosol (Kim et al., 2014; Oishi, Nakaogami, Ishii, & Nagasaki, 2006). Another popular strategy for siRNA attachment on surface functionalized IOMNPs is to establish a cross-linkage by using heterobifunctional cross-linking reagents. For example, N-succinimidyl 3-(2-pyridyldithio)propionate (SPDP) is one of the most commonly used cross-linkers for amine-to-thiol conjugation via amide (the activated N-hydroxysuccinimideester end of SPDP reacts with primary amine groups) and disulfide (the pyridyldithiol group at the other end of SPDP reacts with thiolresidues) linkage (Hermanson, 2013). These newly formed disulfide bonds can be readily broken by intrinsic redox molecules, such as the GSH and thioredoxinin cell, either with or without the enzymes’ assistance (Jere et al., 2009). In addition to SPDP, many of its analogs, including long-chain (LC)-SPDP, Sulfo-LC-SPDP, SMPT (4-succinimidyloxycarbonyl-methyl-α-(2-pyridyldithio)toluene), Sulfo-LC-SMPT, m-maleimidobenzoyl-n-hydroxysuccinimide ester (MBS), Sulfo-MBS, and etc. so forth, have been use for siRNA conjugation applications (Hermanson, 2013). The LC cross-linking agents are designed for greater spatial accessibility, and the sulfonic group modified cross-linking agents may increase the solubility in an aqueous solution.

For therapeutic delivery purposes, the fastest delivery method is via the intravenous route, which maximizes the bioavailability compared with other administration routes. Typically, the IOMNPs-assisted siRNA delivery offers several advantages over the small naked-siRNA molecules on their own, such as: (1) the IOMNPs-assisted delivery approach can protect the siRNAs from RNase degradation in the bloodstream; (2) functionalized siRNA-IOMNPs can increase the blood circulation half-life by reducing the rate of renal clearance; (3) nanoparticle-conjugated siRNAs can ensure the distribution of siRNA-IOMNPs throughout the body; (4) access to the diseased area via passive/active targeting and (5) promoting the cellular uptake and intracellular trafficking; as well as (6) MR imaging-guided siRNA delivery for therapeutic evaluation and disease management. Like other metallic nanoparticles, the pharmacokinetics of siRNA-IOMNPs are determined by their morphology, final HD, surface charge, surface functionalization, protein binding, dose, and animal species (Lin, Monteiro-Riviere, & Riviere, 2015). Based on available evidence, siRNA-loaded metallic nanoparticles without surface engineering tend to accumulate in mononuclear phagocyte system (MPS) organs, including the liver, spleen, blood, bone marrow, and lymph nodes for long periods of time, due to the non-specific uptake of serum protein-bound siRNA-IOMNPs by phagocytic cells, primarily monocytes and macrophages (Song et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2010). Therefore, proper surface functionalization with bio-inert and hydrophilic polymers, including PEG, N-(2-hydroxypropyl)methacrylamide, PEI, PVP, and PVA can prolong circulation time by escaping from the MPS-mediated nonspecific recognition (Kanasty, Dorkin, Vegas, & Anderson, 2013).

The HD of siRNA-IOMNPs is also an important parameter that influences their biodistribution, and it has been consistently found in the size-dependent (~4–100nm) distribution for metallic nanoparticles, with the smaller nanoparticles exhibiting the wider area of distribution, including the liver, spleen, lymph nodes, kidneys, bone marrow, brain, and heart. In addition, the vascular endothelium is negatively charged due to the heparan sulfate proteoglycans that are present on the cellular surface and in the extracellular matrix (Sarrazin, Lamanna, & Esko, 2011), thus the modification of siRNA-IOMNPs with a tunable surface charge can achieve adjustable absorption by tissues and organs. The metabolism of metallic nanoparticles greatly depends on surface coating. Besides, the elimination of metallic nanoparticles is primarily through the biliary and urinary pathways, but a considerable amount of nanoparticles are retained in the liver and spleen.

The specificity and affinity of the siRNA-IOMNPs to targeted diseased organs, tissues, and cells is crucial for effective siRNA delivery (Fig. 9 ). One widely used strategy is the active targeting approach that involves targeting components such as small molecules (folic acid, triphenylphosphonium, and (S)-2-(3-((S)-5-amino-1-carboxypentyl)ureido)pentanedioic acid), peptides, and proteins (arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD), antibodies, aptides, transferrin andaptamers), fatty acids, DNA-targeting sequences, polysaccharides(hyaluronic acid), and so forth (Bertrand, Wu, Xu, Kamaly, & Farokhzad, 2014). It can selectively interact with the substrate binding site of targets (including overexpressed receptors and enzymes in lesion domains) by decorating with an appropriate type of ligand. Therefore, the purpose of using this strategy is to enhance the specific cellular uptake of the therapeutic nanocarriers, and many considerations need to be addressed for the desired delivery efficacy, especially the non-specific binding between nanocarriers and endogenous protein, physicochemical properties (net surface charge, morphology, final HD, and aqueous solubility), and the ligand density.

In addition to active targeting approaches, the passive targeting of nanocarriers to diseased areas (typically tumors) is another potential strategy that is associated with organ-specificity, and it is also governed by certain pathophysiological characteristics of irregular tumor vessels (angiogenesis-promoted fenestrations of capillaries with the pore size ranging from a few hundred nm to several μm), which is known as the effect of MPS, and was previously called the EPR effect (Hume, 2006). Although the concept of the MPS phenomenon can be exploited for passive, targeted nanoparticle-based therapeutic delivery, numerous factors, including the physicochemical properties (morphology, final HD, surface charge, ligand density, protein binding, etc.) of nanocarriers, tumor location, size, and type, as well as the specific microenvironment, can influence the distribution and accumulation of nanocarriers in tumors. For these reasons, a full understanding of the biological behavior of nanomaterials in tumor physiological environments is thus essential to the development of efficient site-specific delivery.

Another attractive targeting method is the EMF-controlled delivery. As mentioned earlier, in this method, a constant EMF is used to transport the therapeutics-loaded IOMNPs to a specific target site within the body. This concept has also been successfully applied to enhance the in vitro gene transfection (magnetofection) for various cell types. Compared with active and passive targeting methods, the major advantage of EMF-controlled delivery is that it reduces both of the undesired systemic distribution methods of the therapeutic IOMNPs and the effective dosage, and enhances the accumulation in target sites by overcoming the biological hindrances such as limited penetration from blood vessels to the tumor site (Lee et al., 2015; Prijic & Sersa, 2011). Based on its physical properties of delivery, this strategy can be applied in various tissues and organs, and the ultimate success in clinical application is counting on the development of high-gradient magnetic field facilities, and high magnetic performance IOMNPs with optimal biocompatibility and safety.

Once the siRNA-loaded IOMNPs reach the target tissue/organ, they have to enter into the target cells for successful delivery of siRNAs and IOMNPs. siRNA-IOMNPs, combined with the aforementioned targeting strategies (i.e., active, passive, and EMF-controlled delivery) can facilitate the cellular uptake through the endocytosis (Fig. 9). In most cases, the positively charged siRNA-IOMNPs can be increasingly taken up by cells because of the negatively charged cell membrane. While for the targeting therapy and imaging, the receptor-mediated cellular internalization using active targeting approaches is often favorable. Usually, the endosomes uptake the siRNA-loaded nanoparticles after the cellular internalization, followed by the fusion with lysosomes, and this entrapment undermines the therapeutic function of siRNAs, which must take place in cytoplasm. One potential strategy designed to enhance the endosomal escape is using cationic polymers (discussed in previous section), which elevates the osmotic pressure and leads to the swelling and rupture of the endosomal and lysosomal membranes.

The in vivo applications of IOMNPs for MR imaging, drug delivery, and combination therapy have been extensively reported (Gao et al., 2013; Liu, Wang, Lee, Ai, & Chen, 2012; Song et al., 2014; Wan et al., 2013; Xing et al., 2013). In the context of IOMNP-based siRNA delivery, the toxicological effects of both IOMNPs and siRNA molecules can no longer be overlooked because the safety issue is one of the major obstacles for clinical translation (Wan et al., 2013). For IOMNPs in general, the nanotoxicity is subject to the nanoparticle surface area-to-volume ratio, chemical stability, composition of the nanomaterial, and amount of dose. In contrast to other metallic nanoparticles, IOMNPs can be degraded into iron ions in liver and spleen tissues as an iron pool, and subsequently transformed to antiferromagnetic ferritin for the needs of various biological processes (Levy et al., 2011). However, a certain high concentration of iron ions in cells can induce oxidative stress owing to free-radical formation, which leads to cell death (van den Bos et al., 2003). It has been demonstrated that possible toxicity from released iron ions can be prevented by adding an effective organic coating, such as polysaccharide, PEG derivatives, and antioxidants to protect the IOMNP surface and improve biocompatibility. For example, dextran-coated IOMNPs are a well-established nanoplatform for imaging and therapeutic delivery; several relevant IOMNPs-based imaging agents such as Resovist® and Feridex® have been approved by the FDA for clinical use (Ros et al., 1995). Although some success has been achieved in clinical translation, the complex physicochemical interaction between IOMNPs and endogenous carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids still need to be elucidated.

The siRNA-based therapeutic safety is another important issue for siRNA-IOMNPs reaching the clinical stage. In some cases, adverse effects may be induced by unsuspected “off-target” silencing through the undesired mRNA knockdown in normal tissues and organs, because of the irrational siRNA design and chemical modification, accompanied by a lack of specificity of the siRNA sequence (Jackson et al., 2006). Besides, excessive length of the siRNA sequence (>30bp) is likely to induce the interferon reaction based on the innate immune system (Hornung et al., 2005; Judge et al., 2005). Thus, a shorter siRNA sequence ranging between 21 and 23bp is highly favorable to avoid sequence-dependent immunostimulatory effects (Hornung et al., 2005). Moreover, the formulation and quality control of siRNA-IOMNPs are also important to govern the nanotoxicity and immunogenicity for systemic delivery. Additionally, the animal model plays a significant role in nanotoxicity investigations. Different animal models may exhibit resultant side effects inconsistently due to the differences at the physiological and anatomical level between species. Therefore, in the approach to clinical translation, the design for good human-like animal models should be necessary. In order to accelerate the in vivo preclinical therapeutic and safety evaluation, the IOMNP-based siRNA delivery can afford real-time MRI monitoring with non-invasive quantitative assessments, including the biophysicochemical processes, and spatial and temporal distribution of siRNA-IOMNPs in vivo (Willmann, van Bruggen, Dinkelborg, & Gambhir, 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular Imaging of IOMNPs-Mediated siRNA Delivery
ce:section.ce:para: The IOMNPs-based siRNA delivery platform has emerged as a promising tool for non-invasive in vivo monitoring of siRNA delivery and silencing by evaluating whole-body biological events on the molecular level in a real-time manner. One of the most important studies has been reported by Medarova et al., their strategy permitted in vivo siRNA delivery with simultaneous MR and NIR imaging of its uptake process in tumors by using a Cy5.5 dye, myristoylated polyarginine peptides, and siRNA labeled IOMNPs (MN-NIRF-siGFP) (Medarova et al., 2007). In this study, the MN-NIRF-siGFP was intravenously injected into two separate mouse models, one with bilaterally implanted 9L-GFP and 9L-RFP tumors, the other bearing subcutaneous LS174T human colorectal adenocarcinoma. A significant drop of T2 relaxivity in the tumors was observed after 24h post-injection, which reflects the accumulation of MN-NIRF-siGFP in tumor tissue and describes the feasibility of in vivo tracking by using an IOMNPs-based siRNA delivery system. In addition, the authors followed the silencing process and corroborated the RNAi efficiency by employing optical imaging (Medarova et al., 2007). Their results indicate that the combination of MR and NIR-guided siRNA delivery in mice can provide sufficient imaging quality and biological information for the development and optimization of an RNAi-based therapeutic product.

Another study demonstrated the use of IOMNPs as a theranostic tool for combined imaging and delivery of siRNA to breast tumors in mice (Kumar, Yigit, Dai, Moore, & Medarova, 2010). In this report, dextran-coated IOMNP cores are triply conjugated to peptide sequences (which specifically interact with tumor-specific antigen uMUC-1), the Cy5.5 dye (for NIR imaging), and siRNA molecules (that binds the tumor-specific antiapoptotic gene BIRC5). This tumor-targeted nanodrug (MN-EPPT-siBIRC5) was then intravenously injected into subcutaneous mouse models bearing human breast tumors, and the in vivo quantitative MRI showed a significant shortening of tumor T2 relaxation times after 24h of injection (Kumar et al., 2010). Furthermore, the NIR imaging showed a high fluorescence signal from tumor site, indicating the preferential tumor uptake of MN-EPPT-siBIRC5, and a significant decrease of the tumor growth rate was observed (Kumar et al., 2010). All data revealed that IOMNPs-based siRNA delivery system illustrates the potential feasibility for clinical studies, because there are several IOMNPs that have already been approved.

In addition to the preceding studies, several relevant investigations have also been carried out to demonstrate the theranostic function of IOMNPs. For example, Lee et al. developed a multifunctional manganese-doped IOMNPs (MnMEIO) coated with bovine serum albumin; its surface is functionalized by a PEG decorated cell-specific targeting ligand (cyclicRGD peptide), and a fluorescent dye (Cy5) labeled siRNA (siGFP) (Lee et al., 2009). In this study, the breast-cancer cells (over express α v β3 integrin) and lung-carcinoma cells (α v β3 integrin deficient) were used as two separate cell lines and treated with MnMEIO-siGFP-Cy5/PEG-RGD nanocomplexes. The T2 contrast MR imaging showed an increased 1/T2 signal intensity in breast-cancer cell lines with rising amounts of the nanoparticles (Lee et al., 2009). No contrast change was observed in lung-carcinoma cell lines, which indicated that these RGD-labeled nanocomplexes can selectively uptake by α v β3 integrin expressed cell lines (Lee et al., 2009). Furthermore, the subcellular distribution and knockdown efficiency of MnMEIO-siGFP-Cy5/PEG-RGD nanocomplexes were monitored by fluorescence imaging ofCy5 dye and GFP expression level (Lee et al., 2009). All results demonstrated that such an imaging-guided siRNA delivery strategy can offer potential benefits, including simultaneous therapeutic treatment and visualization for in vivo and future clinical studies.

One recent attempt has been made to establish an in vivo EMF-controlled and multimodality imaging-guided siRNA delivery for the treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). In this effort, Chen et al. prepared a theranostic IOMNPs-based siRNA delivery strategy by functionalizing a commercial IOMNPs (SilenceMag) with 131I labeled human siVEGF, and following their (131I-hsiVEGF/SilenceMag) subcutaneous injection into HCC tumor-bearing mice, and the injected 131I-hsiVEGF/SilenceMag was magnetically guided to the tumor site by using EMF devices. The biodistribution of 131I-hsiVEGF/SilenceMag was observed by SPECT and MR imaging studies. Both of the imaging modalities indicated a successful attraction and retention of the 131I-hsiVEGF/SilenceMag in HCC tumors. The results demonstrated that both the growth rate of tumor mass and VEGF protein level were deceased in 131I-hsiVEGF/SilenceMag injected mice compared with that of the control group.

The RNAi-based therapeutics remain one of the most revolutionary approaches for personalized treatment of diseases, and currently many siRNA therapeutics are under clinical studies. Although some success has been achieved, there are still many obstacles to systemic delivery of siRNA due to the intrinsic nature of siRNA molecules. IOMNP-based carriers are available for multifunction and effective delivery of siRNAs to the cytoplasm of target cells within the body. In contrast to other nanoplatforms, IOMNPs provide significant advantages, including low toxicity, biodegradability, MRI contrast enhancement, and magnetic force-controlled therapeutic delivery, which could contribute to the applicable theranostic functions that not only affect the development process of siRNA therapeutics, but also intensify disease management.

In order to translate IOMNPs from the laboratory bench to the clinical bedside, additional research is needed to further investigate synthesis of high magnetic performance IOMNPs with low toxicity. The major challenges in pharmacological investigations are the incomplete and inconsistent experimental criteria for pharmacokinetics and the in vivo study of the IOMNPs-based delivery platform, which has resulted in difficulties in integrating and generating a conclusive review from various published experimental data. It has been suggested that the PBPK models could be the best option for pharmacokinetic studies of a variety of metallic nanoparticles (Lin et al., 2015), which can integrate and compare all associated data among different studies to generate more insights into many parameters’ dependent pharmacokinetic outcomes.
